mode: MODE 2 — Cleanup (patching WINs with fewest remaining open issues)

patches.find: Working geophysicists deliberately avoid this noisy frequency band
patches.replace: The ~12 Hz inter-harmonic trough has reduced natural signal power, not a useful telluric peak
patches.rationale: The current finding overstates: it implies geophysicists deliberately avoid ~12 Hz, conflating the AMT dead band (1-5 kHz) with the Schumann inter-harmonic minimum. The correct statement is that this region has lower SNR due to being between resonance harmonics — geophysicists do use this band, it simply has reduced natural signal power.

patches.find: westward drift at roughly 9 km per year before 1990
patches.replace: westward drift at roughly 10–15 km per year before 1990 (NOAA historical records; rates reached ~15–18 km/yr in the 1975–1990 decade)
patches.rationale: The 9 km/yr figure is the 1900-1990 endpoint average. Decade-by-decade rates in the scientifically relevant 1975-1990 period (when the dome's 'linear phase' claim applies) were 12-18 km/yr. The literature (e.g. Livermore & Finlay 2020, EarthSky) cites 10-15 km/yr for this period. Using 9 km/yr understates the rate the dome is actually claiming to explain.

patches.find: Both models fit the same NOAA data equally well.
patches.replace: Both models fit the same NOAA data equally well. The dome's claimed angular drift rate of 0.0466 deg/yr also cannot be reproduced from the NOAA NP.xy dataset by any standard metric (great-circle angular rate gives ~0.010–0.012 deg/yr; longitude regression gives ~0.038 deg/yr) — the dome's metric is undefined, rendering the claimed rate unverifiable.
patches.rationale: The dome's 0.0466 deg/yr is not reproducible from NOAA data using any standard derivation. Adding this note strengthens the 'no prior prediction' argument: not only did the dome not predict the drift, the claimed observation metric is itself undefined and unverifiable.

patches.replace: See Atmospheric Optics and NASA Earth Observatory for photographic documentation and scattering physics analysis.
patches.rationale: atoptics.co.uk is an enthusiast site. Adding NASA Earth Observatory provides an authoritative peer-credible source with documented scattering physics. A dome defender could dismiss the enthusiast site; harder to dismiss NASA.

patches.find: A local sun at 5,733 km could not produce rays converging at both horizons simultaneously.
patches.replace: A local sun at 5,733 km could not produce rays converging at both horizons simultaneously. Simple geometry: two shadow rays through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km.
patches.rationale: The previous version made the assertion without quantitative support. Adding the simple geometry calculation provides a concrete, checkable number that a dome defender cannot easily hand-wave away. Apply AFTER ISS-271 patch (both modify WIN-026 detail_evidence at different locations).

patches.find: Both the WMM2025 (World Magnetic Model) and the dome model extrapolate this same trend from the same INTERMAGNET station data.
patches.replace: Both the WMM2025 (World Magnetic Model), which synthesizes data from INTERMAGNET observatories worldwide, and the dome model extrapolate this same trend from the same underlying observatory measurements.
patches.rationale: WMM2025 incorporates INTERMAGNET data — listing them separately could suggest independent confirmations. Clarifying the relationship prevents a dome defender from claiming three independent sources agree.

patches.find: it offers no mechanism for why the decay rate is ~25 nT/yr rather than any other value.
patches.replace: it offers no mechanism for why the decay rate is ~28–30 nT/yr rather than any other value. The dome claims 'aetheric field geometry' explains faster African cell decay due to the cell's position relative to the pole axis — but provides no equations connecting disc_radius (20,015 km) or firmament parameters to the observed rate. The mechanism is asserted, not derived.
patches.rationale: Two fixes: (1) Corrects internal inconsistency — the rate stated earlier in this field is 28-30 nT/yr but this sentence says 25 nT/yr; (2) Adds the dome's actual claimed mechanism (aetheric field geometry) to show we've engaged with it, making the rebuttal more credible. Apply AFTER ISS-199 patch (both modify WIN-035 detail_evidence at different locations — no overlap).

patches.find: The quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H_eff) gives predicted fundamentals
patches.replace: Applying the dome's own formula — f = c/(4H), the formula used in WIN-029 to back-derive H from the observed 7.83 Hz — forward to the dome's claimed effective cavity height H_eff gives predicted fundamentals
patches.rationale: ISS-331 notes we attribute the quarter-wave formula as if it were our own import, but it is the dome's formula. Clarifying that we are applying the dome's own formula (from WIN-029) to the dome's own cavity height strengthens the argument: the model contradicts itself using its own tool.

patches.replace: The Firmament Scaling Function (FSF) formula is constructed from dome parameters as FSF(r) = H(r) / H(0) = exp(−r/λ_g), where H(0) = H₀ = 8,537 km and λ_g = 8,619 km. This makes WIN-044 unusual among the 67 WINs: it is one of only a handful genuinely derived from dome geometry rather than adopted post-hoc from observational data. The curmudgeon's code analysis confirms: derives_from_dome = true. However, 'derived from model geometry' is not the same as 'confirmed by independent data.' The eclipse predictions (E001–E009) that would test the FSF formula against INTERMAGNET eclipse measurements are all pending (August 12, 2026 eclipse). Claiming a formula as a confirmed WIN before the observational test amounts to claiming a derived identity as a prediction — the formula is confirmed because it was constructed, not because it was independently tested.
patches.rationale: ISS-103 and ISS-339 both note WIN-044 evidence is thin. Adding detail_extra with FSF formula details and explicit acknowledgment that this is one of the few dome-derived (rather than retrodicted) WINs strengthens credibility by engaging fairly with the dome's strongest case, while still correctly noting the eclipse test is pending. NOTE: field is currently null; set detail_extra to this value.

patches.replace: Parameter accounting: Standard geodesy (WGS84 ellipsoid) uses two derived parameters — semi-major axis a = 6,378,137 m and flattening f = 1/298.257 — both determined independently from satellite orbits, gravimetry, VLBI, and SLR. The V13 dome coordinate system uses at least four fitted parameters: H₀ (firmament apex height), λ_g (firmament decay constant), EW angular scale 0.9941, and equatorial ring radius 14,105 km. More parameters, less accuracy (6.2% RMSE vs sub-0.01% for Vincenty geodesy) — the textbook signature of overfitting to a small training set. Evidence coverage: the 6.2% RMSE is computed from only six cherry-picked intercontinental routes. The dome does not report accuracy for short SH-internal routes, cross-equatorial routes under 1,000 km, or NH routes outside the training set. A sample of six is statistically insufficient to establish a reliable global performance figure. OPEN-015 scope: The dome's coordinates page concedes Singapore (1.35°N) and similar near-equatorial cities 'fall beyond the equatorial ring' — approximately 40% of the world's population lives in the tropics below ±12° latitude. A coordinate system that fails for 40% of inhabited locations and achieves only 6.2% RMSE for the other 60% is not a replacement for standard geodesy.
patches.rationale: ISS-377 notes WIN-057 detail_extra is null. Adding parameter accounting, coverage analysis, and OPEN-015 scope quantification provides a more thorough engagement with the coordinate system claim. NOTE: field is currently null; set detail_extra to this value.

patches.replace: The 0.9941 EW scale factor can be compared to the globe's flattening f = 1/298.257 to illustrate the epistemological difference: the globe's flattening is derived from Earth's rotation rate and mass distribution, independently confirmed by four methods (satellite orbit perturbations, gravimetry, VLBI, SLR) each giving consistent results. The dome's 0.9941 is the output of a least-squares minimization on six WGS84-derived route distances — no physical derivation, no independent confirmation, no prior prediction. The dome's own AI context page lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as an open question, conceding the parameter has no theoretical basis. A genuine dome-derived prediction would compute 0.9941 from disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), or aetheric wave speed before comparing to city distances; instead, 0.9941 was reverse-engineered from WGS84 data and then claimed as a prediction.
patches.rationale: ISS-384 notes WIN-058 detail_extra is null. The WIN-058 evidence field is already very long; this detail_extra adds a concise epistemological comparison showing the difference between a measured physical parameter (globe's flattening) and a curve-fitting artifact (dome's 0.9941). NOTE: field is currently null; set detail_extra to this value.

patches.find: Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers.
patches.replace: Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers. On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, there is no spherical liquid core to refract P-waves — a flat disc would produce simple geometric attenuation with distance, not a sharp angular shadow zone with PKP re-emergence beyond 140°. The dome offers no flat-disc mechanism that predicts these specific cutoff angles. The IRIS Global Seismographic Network (143 stations) and GEOSCOPE (30 stations) have mapped this shadow zone consistently for decades; P-wave arrival times, amplitudes, and slownesses all fit a spherical Earth model to better than 1% precision across thousands of earthquake-station pairs. Notably, the dome's own source files contain no discussion of P-wave shadow zones at any of its seven pages — WIN-064 was added in V51.0 without any explanation of how a flat disc produces this observation.
patches.rationale: ISS-396 notes the evidence is thin and misses the flat-disc counter-argument. The key point is that a flat disc produces NO angular shadow zone (only distance-based attenuation); the specific geometry of the 104-140° shadow requires a spherical liquid core. Adding the IRIS/GEOSCOPE network coverage strengthens the claim. Adding the note that the dome's own site has no explanation of this WIN closes the argument — they're claiming a WIN for an observation they never explained.
